content:
# Genesis Chapter 36 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20v–21v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_036_page_002.jpg`, `GEN_036_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037 (page 33), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0038 (page 34), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0039 (page 35)

---

## The Generations of Esau — The Family Tree of Edom

**1** These are the generations of Esau — that is, Edom.

---

## Esau's Wives and Sons Born in Canaan

**2** Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite; Oholibamah daughter of Anah — the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite; **3** and Basemath, daughter of Ishmael and sister of Nebaioth.

**4** Adah bore Esau a son: Eliphaz. Basemath bore Reuel. **5** Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These were the sons of Esau, born to him in the land of Canaan.

---

## Esau Moves to Seir — The Separation from Jacob

**6** Then Esau took his wives, his sons and daughters, and all the members of his household — his livestock, all his animals, and everything he had accumulated in Canaan — and moved away from his brother Jacob. **7** Their combined possessions were simply too great for them to live together; the land where they were staying could not support them both because of their livestock. **8** So Esau settled in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom.

---

## The Generations of Esau in the Hill Country of Seir

**9** These are the generations of Esau, the father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir.

**10** These are the names of Esau's sons:

Eliphaz, son of Adah, Esau's wife; and Reuel, son of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**11** The sons of Eliphaz:

Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.

**12** Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore him Amalek. These are the descendants of Adah, Esau's wife.

**13** These are the sons of Reuel:

Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These are the descendants of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**14** These are the sons of Oholibamah daughter of Anah — the granddaughter of Zibeon — Esau's wife: She bore Esau: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.

---

## The Chiefs Who Descended from Esau

**15** These are the chiefs among Esau's descendants:

The sons of Eliphaz, Esau's firstborn: Chief Teman, Chief Omar, Chief Zepho, Chief Kenaz, **16** Chief Korah, Chief Gatam, Chief Amalek. These are the chiefs descended from Eliphaz in the land of Edom — all of them descendants of Adah.

**17** These are the sons of Reuel, Esau's son: Chief Nahath, Chief Zerah, Chief Shammah, Chief Mizzah. These are the chiefs descended from Reuel in the land of Edom — all of them descendants of Basemath, Esau's wife.

**18** These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife: Chief Jeush, Chief Jalam, Chief Korah. These are the chiefs descended from Oholibamah daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.

**19** These are the sons of Esau — and these are their chiefs. He is Edom.

---

## The Horites — The Original People of Seir

**20** These are the sons of Seir the Horite, who were the original inhabitants of the land:

Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, **21** Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, the sons of Seir in the land of Edom.

**22** The sons of Lotan: Hori and Hemam. And Lotan's sister was Timna.

**23** The sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.

**24** The sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah — this is the Anah who found the hot springs in the wilderness while he was grazing the donkeys of his father Zibeon.

**25** The children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah daughter of Anah.

**26** The sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.

**27** The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.

**28** The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.

**29** These are the chiefs of the Horites: Chief Lotan, Chief Shobal, Chief Zibeon, Chief Anah, **30** Chief Dishon, Chief Ezer, Chief Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, clan by clan in the land of Seir.

---

## The Kings of Edom — Before Israel Had Any King

**31** These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel:

**32** Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom; the name of his city was Dinhabah.

**33** When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place.

**34** When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.

**35** When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad — who defeated Midian in the fields of Moab — reigned in his place. The name of his city was Avith.

**36** When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah reigned in his place.

**37** When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth-on-the-River reigned in his place.

**38** When Shaul died, Baal-hanan son of Achbor reigned in his place.

**39** When Baal-hanan son of Achbor died, Hadar reigned in his place. The name of his city was Pau, and his wife's name was Mehetabel daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-zahab.

---

## The Final Catalogue of Edomite Chiefs

**40** These are the names of the chiefs descended from Esau, by their clans, their territories, and their names: Chief Timna, Chief Alvah, Chief Jetheth, **41** Chief Oholibamah, Chief Elah, Chief Pinon, **42** Chief Kenaz, Chief Teman, Chief Mibzar, **43** Chief Magdiel, Chief Iram.

These are the chiefs of Edom, according to their settlements in the land they possessed.

This is Esau, the father of Edom.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Why Genesis honors the other brother

It is a question worth sitting with: why does the book of Genesis devote an entire chapter to Esau? He lost the birthright. He sold it for stew. He lost the blessing through his brother's deception. He swore to kill Jacob; Jacob fled; and for twenty years the brothers lived apart. When they finally reunite in chapter 33, the reunion is brief and surprisingly warm — but Jacob almost immediately keeps moving, and Esau returns to Seir. He is, in narrative terms, the "other" brother — the one who did not receive the covenant promises, the one through whom the story does not run.

And yet Genesis 36 exists. Forty-three verses. Six hundred years of Edomite royal history, three full catalogues of chiefs, a list of pre-Israelite indigenous inhabitants, an entire political genealogy of a neighboring nation — all given to the man who was not chosen.

This is not an accident. The theological logic of Genesis consistently honors those who stand alongside the covenant line without being included in it. Abraham has Ishmael; Isaac has Esau. In each case, the non-chosen son receives a blessing of a different kind: Ishmael becomes twelve princes (25:16); Esau becomes a nation of chiefs and kings. The election of one does not nullify the dignity of the other. Esau is blessed — abundantly, richly, historically — just not with the specific covenant through which the whole earth will be blessed (12:3).

Genesis 36 is the text's way of saying: the story goes this way, through Jacob/Israel — but God has not forgotten the other brother. His line is real. His children have names. His territory has borders. His kings have biographies. He matters.

---

### Verses 1 and 8 — "Esau, that is, Edom"

The double identification **עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם** — "Esau, that is, Edom" — appears three times in this chapter (vv.1, 8, 19) and once more at the very end (v.43: "Esau, the father of Edom"). It is an editorial refrain, a deliberate and insistent equation. Esau *is* Edom. The man and the nation are one and the same thing. The formula works in both directions: to humanize Edom (these are not a faceless foreign people; they descend from a man we know, from a family we have followed) and to historicize Esau (every subsequent Israelite encounter with Edom is, at some level, an echo of the Jacob-Esau story).

This matters enormously for how Israel was supposed to think about Edom. The Edomites were not strangers; they were cousins. The Mosaic law will later reflect this: "You shall not abhor an Edomite, for he is your brother" (Deuteronomy 23:7). Genesis 36 provides the genealogical ground for that command. These are the descendants of your uncle. You share a grandfather.

The repeated identification also does something more subtle: it fixes Esau's identity permanently at this point in the narrative. Genesis 36 is the formal closing of Esau's story. After this chapter, Esau does not appear again. He has been named, his family documented, his territory established, and now he recedes from the stage of the primary narrative. The equation "Esau = Edom" is the text's way of saying: his story is now a nation's story, and that nation's story is a different story from the one Genesis is following.

---

### Verses 2-3 — The wife-name discrepancies

Genesis 36 gives Esau three wives: Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite, Oholibamah daughter of Anah, and Basemath daughter of Ishmael. But Genesis 26:34 and 28:9 gave different names: Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Mahalath daughter of Ishmael. The names of the wives have shifted. Basemath in chapter 26 is the Hittite woman (daughter of Elon); in chapter 36 Basemath is the Ishmaelite woman (daughter of Ishmael). Judith and Mahalath have vanished entirely, replaced by Adah and Oholibamah.

This is one of the oldest cruxes in biblical scholarship. Proposed solutions range widely: the women had multiple names (common in the ancient Near East); the sources behind Genesis 36 and the earlier chapters are independent traditions that have not been harmonized; the names reflect different clan identifications as women moved between families; or there were in fact more than three wives and the lists represent different stages of the household. The Leningrad Codex (our primary source) presents the chapter-36 names without any apparent awareness of a contradiction — and this is itself significant. For the Masoretic scribal tradition, there was no discrepancy requiring correction.

What the discrepancies actually tell us is something true and important about the nature of this text: it is the deposit of a long oral and scribal tradition, carrying multiple streams of memory. The names of wives in ancient genealogies could shift with clan allegiance, with marriage customs, with the perspective of the community preserving the memory. The text is not a modern biography. It is the record of a people's memory of a people, and memory of this kind preserves the outline and the weight of the tradition even when the specific details have blurred over time. PNEUMA follows the Leningrad Codex text for chapter 36 throughout, noting but not harmonizing the earlier discrepancies, because the tradition itself chose not to harmonize them.

---

### Verses 6-8 — The separation: a pattern in Genesis

**וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל-אֶרֶץ מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו** — "and he went away from before his brother Jacob." Esau's departure to Seir because "their possessions were too great" to coexist is an almost word-for-word echo of the Abraham-Lot separation in Genesis 13:

- Genesis 13:6: "the land could not support them both, for their possessions were great" (**כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב**)
- Genesis 36:7: "their possessions were too great to dwell together" (**כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב**)

The verbal echo is exact. The narrator wants us to hear it. This is not coincidence; it is literary architecture. Genesis has a recurring pattern: two men who are deeply connected — uncle and nephew, brothers — find that the land cannot hold them both, and one departs so that the other can remain. Lot goes toward Sodom; Esau goes to Seir. In both cases, the one who departs is making room — whether he knows it or not — for the covenant narrative to continue unimpeded.

There is something generous in how Genesis frames these departures. Neither Lot nor Esau is driven away in disgrace. They go because the land is full. The separation is practical before it is theological. But the theological consequence is the same: the stage is cleared for the story God is telling. Abraham remains in Canaan; Jacob remains in Canaan. The promise runs through the one who stays.

What makes the Esau separation poignant — more poignant than the Abraham-Lot separation — is the backstory of Jacob and Esau. These are twins. They shared a womb and twenty years of family strife. Jacob cheated Esau twice. And yet when they reunite in chapter 33, Esau runs to meet Jacob and embraces him and weeps. The enmity is over. And then — quietly, without drama — Esau packs up his household and moves to Seir. He does not need to be expelled. He goes. It is, in its way, the most gracious thing Esau ever does for his brother.

---

### Verse 12 — Amalek

**וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ לֶאֱלִיפַז בֶּן-עֵשָׂו וַתֵּלֶד לֶאֱלִיפַז אֶת-עֲמָלֵק** — "And Timna was a concubine to Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore Amalek to Eliphaz."

One verse. Tucked inside a genealogical list. And in it, the ancestor of Israel's most implacable enemy.

Amalek — the Amalekites — will attack Israel from behind at Rephidim while they are exhausted in the wilderness (Exodus 17:8-16), striking the weakest and most vulnerable. God's response is the most absolute declaration in the Torah: "I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven" (Exodus 17:14). Moses holds up his hands throughout the battle. Joshua fights. The LORD fights. And afterward, God declares perpetual war with Amalek across generations (Exodus 17:16). The Amalekite king Agag will haunt Saul's reign (1 Samuel 15). Haman, who nearly annihilates the Jewish people in the book of Esther, is identified as an Agagite — from the Amalekite royal line.

And here, in Genesis 36:12, the text gives us the genealogical root: Amalek is the son of Esau's son Eliphaz, by a concubine named Timna. He is Esau's grandson. He is Jacob's great-nephew.

The notation that Timna was a *concubine* (pilegesh) rather than a wife carries status implications: Amalek is the child of a secondary union, a grandson of the second-generation line, a son born at the edge of the household. And yet from that peripheral birth comes the people who will define Israel's wilderness suffering. Genesis places this lineage quietly inside Esau's genealogy — not as a climax, not with editorial alarm, but as a simple genealogical datum. The reader who knows the rest of the story reads it with a shudder.

Note also: the Leningrad Codex text is careful to place Timna within the genealogical structure as Eliphaz's concubine, distinguishing her status from that of a wife. The chapter later tells us (v.22) that Timna was also the *sister* of Lotan, who was a Horite chief — meaning she came from the indigenous Horite population of Seir and entered the Edomite family line through Eliphaz. She is a woman from a conquered or absorbed people, and her son Amalek becomes one of the most feared names in the Hebrew Bible. The genealogy does not explain this trajectory. It simply records it, trusting the reader to carry the freight of what the name will come to mean.

---

### Verses 15-19 and 40-43 — The allufim: chiefs, clans, or something else?

The word **אַלּוּף** (*alluf*) appears forty times in Genesis 36 — more than in the rest of the Hebrew Bible combined. It is the chapter's distinctive political vocabulary. The root is related to *eleph* (thousand, or clan-group), suggesting leadership of a group of a thousand, or perhaps chieftainship of a clan unit. In this chapter it describes the leaders of Esau's descendants and the Horite chiefs alike — a governing structure specific to the Edomite and Horite political world that differs from the Israelite tribal system.

The LXX Vaticanus renders *alluf* as **ἡγεμών** (*hegemon*) — leader, ruler, commander. This is a reasonable equivalent: a *hegemon* in Greek usage was a military commander, a territorial chief, someone who holds authority over a defined region. The word appears in the Vaticanus text of Genesis 36 with the same frequency as *alluf* in the MT. PNEUMA translates *alluf* as "chief" throughout — a word that captures the combination of political authority and clan identity that *alluf* implies, without importing the later biblical terminology of judge or king.

The structure of the alluf lists is worth noticing. The first list (vv.15-19) organizes Esau's chiefs by matriline — the sons of Adah, the sons of Basemath, the sons of Oholibamah — giving each wife's branch its own catalogue and ending each with the woman's name. This matrilineal organization within a patriarchal genealogy is unusual, and it suggests that the three wives' households maintained distinct identities within the broader Edomite political structure. The final catalogue (vv.40-43) reorganizes the chiefs geographically — "by their clans, their territories, and their names" — suggesting that this list reflects a later stage of territorial settlement, when the family lines have diversified into regional political units.

The two systems of organization (by matriline, then by territory) present the same people in two different ways, as if the narrator wants to show both the family structure and its eventual political geography.

---

### Verses 20-30 — The Horites: the people who were there first

**אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-שֵׂעִיר הַחֹרִי יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ** — "These are the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land."

The phrase **יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ** — "inhabitants of the land," "those who dwelt in the land" — is the standard biblical designation for indigenous peoples who occupied a territory before a later group arrived. Here it identifies the Horites as the population Esau's family found in Seir, the people already living in the hill country before the Edomites established dominance.

Who were the Horites? The name has sometimes been connected to the Hurrians, a major Bronze Age people of northern Mesopotamia and Syria, but the linguistic link is uncertain and the geographical overlap is limited. More likely, "Horite" refers to a distinct Transjordanian people — perhaps cave-dwellers (*hor* in Hebrew means cave) — who inhabited the rocky highlands of Seir before the Edomite expansion. Deuteronomy 2:12 makes explicit what Genesis 36 implies: "The Horites formerly lived in Seir, but the descendants of Esau dispossessed them and destroyed them from before them and settled in their place, as Israel did to the land of their possession."

Genesis 36 gives the Horite chiefs their own genealogical catalogue — not in a subordinate list appended to Esau's, but in their own distinct section with its own introduction and its own closing summary (v.30). The text treats them with the same genealogical dignity it extends to Esau's descendants. They have a father — Seir — and seven sons, and each son has his own children. They are a people, not merely a backdrop. The narrator honors them with names even as the historical record implies that they were absorbed, displaced, or destroyed by the Edomite expansion.

There is one startling connection: Oholibamah, Esau's third wife, is "daughter of Anah, granddaughter of Zibeon" — and Anah and Zibeon both appear in the Horite genealogy (vv.20,24). This means Esau married into the Horite ruling class. His union with Oholibamah is a cross-ethnic marriage — an Edomite patriarch joining his household to a Horite family. The descendants of that union (Jeush, Jalam, and Korah, v.5) become Edomite chiefs (v.18) who carry both bloodlines. The absorption of the Horites into Edom was not purely military; it also happened through marriage, as it usually does.

---

### Verse 24 — The hot springs: what the yemim were

**הוּא עֲנָה אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת-הַיֵּמִם בַּמִּדְבָּר** — "He is the Anah who found the yemim in the wilderness."

**הַיֵּמִם** (*hayyemim*) is one of the most disputed hapax legomena in Genesis. The word appears only here in the Hebrew Bible, and nobody is entirely certain what it means.

The ancient translations diverge significantly. The Vulgate renders it **aquas calidas** — "hot springs" or "warm waters." The Targum Onkelos reads it as **gibbore'in** — "mighty ones," "giants," or "warriors." The Targum Pseudo-Jonathan adds a more colorful tradition: Anah found a new breed of animal — mules, produced by crossing horses with donkeys — in the wilderness, introducing a transgressive hybrid creature into the world. The LXX Vaticanus renders it **τοὺς Ἰαμίν** — "the Iamin" — essentially transliterating the Hebrew rather than translating it, which suggests the LXX translator also did not know what the word meant.

The Masoretic tradition preserved this word with its full Tiberian vocalization — clearly distinguishable from any of the common words it might be confused with — without supplying an explanatory gloss, which is itself notable. The scribes of the Leningrad Codex transmitted what they received without smoothing it.

PNEUMA translates "hot springs" — following the Vulgate and the most common scholarly reading today — while acknowledging that the word's meaning is genuinely uncertain. What is certain is that the notation singles out Anah as a man distinguished by an unusual discovery. The detail is preserved because it was remembered — a fragment of biographical tradition about a Horite chief, embedded in a genealogy, surviving because the memory of this one strange fact was strong enough to persist.

---

### Verses 31-39 — Kings before kings: the editorial window into the future

**וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** — "And these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel."

This is the most theologically remarkable verse in Genesis 36, and one of the most theologically remarkable clauses in the entire book of Genesis. The narrator casually reaches forward in time — past the whole of Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy, Joshua, Judges — to the moment when Israel finally asks for a king (1 Samuel 8), and he calibrates the Edomite king list against that future event.

He knows it is coming. He writes from a vantage point that assumes Israel will eventually have kings. He does not say when. He does not say who. He simply uses the existence of that future Israelite monarchy as a chronological reference point: Edom had kings *before* Israel did. The clause is simultaneously a piece of historical information (Edom had an early and apparently successful monarchic tradition) and a theological observation (the "other" brother's nation was politically sophisticated before the chosen nation was).

This is the narrator of Genesis breaking the narrative frame to speak from historical hindsight — not for the last time (cf. "to this day" phrases throughout Genesis), but here with unusual explicitness. The anachronism is deliberate. It is an invitation to the reader to understand that the book of Genesis is not naive about where history goes; it is written in full awareness of the long story that follows, and this genealogy is placed within that awareness.

The Edomite king list itself is distinctive: eight kings are named, and — unlike Israelite and most ancient Near Eastern dynastic lists — no king is succeeded by his son. Each king appears to come from a different city or region: Dinhabah, Bozrah, Teman, Avith, Masrekah, Rehoboth, Pau. This suggests that Edom's early monarchy was not dynastic in the familiar sense but rather a shifting chieftainship — perhaps a rotating kingship among the leading clans, perhaps an elective or conquest-based system. The kings are bound together not by bloodline but by territory and title. Edom is a monarchy, but not yet a dynasty.

1 Chronicles 1:43-54 reproduces this king list with only one significant variant: the final king is called "Hadad" in Chronicles (matching most manuscripts of the MT reading "Hadar" in Genesis 36:39). The Leningrad Codex reads **הֲדַר** (*Hadar*) in verse 39; the LXX Vaticanus reads **Αδαδ** (*Adad*, i.e., Hadad). This textual variant is ancient — it appears already in the LXX tradition — and Chronicles represents the correction toward "Hadad," a name that appears frequently among Edomite and Aramean rulers throughout the Hebrew Bible. The MT's "Hadar" is the more difficult reading and may be the earlier one; "Hadad" looks like a harmonization with the more familiar name. PNEUMA follows the Leningrad Codex and translates "Hadar."

---

### The pattern of separation: theological geography

Genesis has a recurring spatial logic: the covenant runs through one line, and the other lines move away. Not driven away — in each case the separation is practically motivated and often mutually agreeable — but the movement always goes in the same direction: away from the center, away from Canaan, away from the place where the covenant operates.

Abraham sends Ishmael east, to the wilderness (21:14); Ishmael's descendants "settled from Havilah to Shur, east of Egypt" (25:18). Abraham sends Keturah's sons east, "away from his son Isaac" (25:6). Lot moves toward Sodom, east of the Jordan (13:11). And now Esau moves to Seir — southeast of Canaan, into the hill country beyond the Dead Sea.

The theological geography is not accidental. Canaan is the land of promise, the place where God's story with Israel will unfold. The departures of Ishmael, Lot, and Esau clear the stage. They do not diminish these figures — their stories, their children, their nations are honored with genealogical detail. But they mark the boundary of the central story, the line between where the covenant narrative runs and where the parallel narratives of other peoples unfold beyond its edge.

What makes Esau's departure particularly poignant is that he goes last. Abraham's time is long past. Isaac is dead. And now the final separation of the twin brothers — the separation that was prophesied before their birth ("two nations are in your womb," 25:23) — is settled not by drama but by pragmatics: there is not enough room for both of them. So Esau goes. He packs his enormous household and his prodigious flocks and he moves to Seir, and Genesis 36 is the testimony to what he built there — a civilization of chiefs and kings, of clans and territories, of a people who knew their own names and remembered their own founding families.

He is Edom. And Genesis honors him with this: forty-three verses of memory.

---

### The woman named Timna — a story on the edge

There are two Timnas in Genesis 36. The first is Timna the concubine of Eliphaz, mother of Amalek (v.12). The second is Timna the sister of Lotan, daughter of the Horite chief (v.22). These may or may not be the same woman — the text does not say. But placing both notices in the same chapter invites the reader to consider the possibility that they are.

If Timna of v.22 (Horite, daughter of Seir's line) is the same Timna of v.12 (concubine to Esau's son Eliphaz), then her story is this: she was born into the indigenous Horite ruling class, became a concubine in the Edomite household that was absorbing her people's territory, and bore the child who would become the ancestor of the nation Israel most feared in the wilderness. She moved from one people to another through the institution of concubinage — a secondary status, not a wife's status — and her son's legacy would be defined by violence.

A later rabbinic tradition (Sanhedrin 99b) fills in a motive for Timna's choice: she had sought to convert and join the Abrahamic family — approaching Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — and all three had refused her. So she became a concubine to the house of Esau instead, reasoning that it was better to be a servant in the household of this family than a princess in another. The tradition adds: because the patriarchs rejected her, Israel was punished through her son Amalek. The rabbinic imagination uses Timna's story to raise a moral question about exclusion — who is excluded from the covenant community, by whom, and what are the consequences?

Genesis does not tell this story. It does not explain Timna's choice or her origins or her fate. It names her concubine, names her son, notes her brother's name — and moves on. But the two mentions of Timna in this chapter, close enough together to create a gravitational pull, leave a story that the reader can feel even if they cannot fully see it. She is a woman at the edge of the great genealogy, unnamed in its chieftain lists, and her son's name is the most haunting inheritance in the whole chapter.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20v–21v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Page 001 (folio 20v, PDF page 39) carries the final verses of Genesis 35 in its upper section, with the Masoretic parasha section marker visible before the opening of Genesis 36. The toledot formula of verse 1 — **אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם** — is clearly legible with full Tiberian vowel pointing and cantillation. The three-column format of the LC carries the wives' names in verses 2-3 in the first column, with the initial sons-list running through approximately verse 14. The name **עֲמָלֵק** (Amalek) in verse 12 is distinctly pointed in the manuscript. Page 002 (folio 21r, PDF page 40) carries the chiefs (*allufim*) catalogues of verses 15-30, with the repeated title **אַלּוּף** clearly visible throughout the right and center columns. The Horite genealogy begins in the center of this folio, with the introductory formula **בְנֵי-שֵׂעִיר הַחֹרִי יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ** legible near the middle of the page. The difficult hapax **הַיֵּמִם** in verse 24 appears with full Masoretic vocalization — pointing that distinguishes it from common words — and no scribal gloss. The king list of verses 31-39 begins near the bottom of this folio, with the remarkable clause **לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** visible on the manuscript. Page 003 (folio 21v, PDF page 41) carries the continuation of the king list through verse 39, the final chiefs catalogue of verses 40-43, and the closing verse — **עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם** — before the opening of Genesis 37. The name **הֲדַר** (Hadar) in verse 39 is pointed as in the standard Leningrad Codex tradition; the Masoretic text preserves the *daleth* where the Chronicles tradition has the more common form Hadad. The parasha section marker at the close of Genesis 36 and the opening of Genesis 37 is visible at the end of the third column. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 33–35 (pa_0033–pa_0035; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037, /0038, /0039). Page 33 (pa_0033) carries the transition from Genesis 35 into Genesis 36, with the chapter heading visible in the right margin. Page 34 (pa_0034) carries the bulk of the Genesis 36 material through the king list, with **CAP. XLI** visible as a chapter marker at the top of the page — corresponding approximately to the second section of our material (the Vaticanus chapter divisions do not map exactly onto the MT chapter divisions). Page 35 (pa_0035) carries the conclusion of Genesis 36 and the opening of Genesis 37, with **CAP. XLII** at the top and **CAP. XLIII** visible in the right margin marking the beginning of the Genesis 37 material. Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants: v.2 — Vaticanus renders Oholibamah's lineage as "daughter of Anah son of Zibeon the Horite" (ἐκ τῆς Ανα θυγατρὸς Σεβεγων τοῦ Χορραίου), describing Anah as son rather than identifying Oholibamah as granddaughter of Zibeon, which slightly reorganizes the family tree; v.24 — Vaticanus transliterates *hayyemim* as **τοὺς Ἰαμίν** rather than translating it, confirming that the word was opaque to the Greek translators; v.39 — Vaticanus reads **Αδαδ** (Hadad) where the Leningrad Codex reads **הֲדַר** (Hadar), the same variant preserved in 1 Chronicles 1:50; the LXX also uniquely specifies Hadar's wife Mehetabel as "daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezaab" (matching the MT), suggesting that this detailed notice was already present in the Greek Vorlage. DSS: No significant Genesis 36 fragments are extant from the Dead Sea Scrolls materials.*

*— PNEUMA*
